title: UN Security Council Convenes Emergency Session Over Gaza Ceasefire Collapse
summary: World leaders demand accountability as fragile truce breaks down along the Gaza corridor, leaving humanitarian aid convoys stranded and millions at risk.
content:
The United Nations Security Council convened an emergency session on Monday after a fragile ceasefire in Gaza broke down overnight, with both Israeli forces and Hamas-aligned factions trading accusations of initiating the renewed hostilities that have killed dozens and displaced thousands in the span of hours.

Ambassadors from France, the United Kingdom, and the United States delivered urgent statements calling for an immediate return to negotiations, while Russia and China jointly demanded an independent international inquiry into the collapse of the truce brokered just eleven days earlier under Egyptian mediation.

"The international community cannot afford another cycle of escalation," UN Secretary-General António Guterres said in opening remarks. "Every hour of delay costs lives that cannot be recovered."

Aid organizations operating in the territory reported that at least fourteen humanitarian convoys have been stranded since the resumption of hostilities, with access routes around the Rafah and Kerem Shalom crossings sealed by military operations. The World Food Programme estimates that over 2.3 million people in Gaza remain critically dependent on external food supplies.

Diplomatic sources indicated that Egypt and Qatar are continuing behind-the-scenes efforts to restore the ceasefire, with a proposed 72-hour pause under discussion. However, no formal agreement had been reached as of press time.

The session concluded without a binding resolution after the United States exercised its veto power over a draft text that would have demanded an immediate and permanent ceasefire, calling the language "unenforceable" without greater accountability mechanisms. The vote drew sharp criticism from the Arab League and the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation, both of which issued statements condemning the outcome.

title: G20 Summit in Johannesburg Closes With Landmark Climate Finance Pact
summary: Developed nations pledge $500 billion over a decade to support green transitions in emerging economies, though critics call the timeline dangerously slow.
content:
The G20 summit in Johannesburg concluded on Sunday with the signing of a landmark climate finance agreement under which wealthy member states committed to mobilizing $500 billion over ten years to support clean energy transitions and climate adaptation programs in developing and emerging economies.

The deal, which was brokered over three days of intensive negotiations, represents the most substantial multilateral climate finance commitment since the Paris Agreement and was hailed by South African President Cyril Ramaphosa, who hosted the summit, as a turning point in the global response to the climate crisis.

However, environmental groups and several developing nation delegations expressed frustration that the bulk of the financing would come in the form of loans rather than grants, arguing that debt-laden nations could not afford to borrow their way to a green economy. The distinction matters enormously for countries like Bangladesh, which faces existential climate risks but carries a debt-to-GDP ratio that limits its capacity for additional borrowing.

The United States and European Union framed the agreement as a pragmatic compromise that could be implemented immediately, while China announced a separate bilateral initiative to invest in renewable energy infrastructure across fifteen African nations.

The summit also produced agreements on digital taxation, supply chain resilience, and a new framework for regulating cross-border artificial intelligence deployment — though the latter remains subject to national ratification processes that analysts expect to be lengthy and contentious.

title: India-Pakistan Diplomatic Channel Reopens After Three-Year Freeze
summary: Back-channel talks facilitated by Oman have yielded a provisional agreement to resume consular services and ease trade restrictions along the Wagah border.
content:
India and Pakistan have agreed to reopen diplomatic channels frozen since the 2023 Pahalgam crisis, following months of back-channel talks facilitated by Oman that have yielded a provisional framework for resuming consular services and easing certain trade restrictions at the Wagah-Attari border crossing.

The agreement, confirmed by foreign ministry spokespersons in both New Delhi and Islamabad on Thursday, falls well short of a full normalization of relations but represents the most significant diplomatic development on the subcontinent in years. Both sides described the talks as preliminary and cautioned against overstating the significance of the breakthrough.

Under the terms of the provisional arrangement, both countries will reinstate visa processing for a limited set of humanitarian categories — including medical travel, family reunification, and religious pilgrimages — within 60 days. Trade in a restricted list of commodities will resume through official land crossing channels, replacing the informal routes that have operated throughout the freeze.

Regional analysts credited Oman's Foreign Minister Badr bin Hamad Al-Busaidi with engineering the talks, leveraging Muscat's longstanding relationships with both capitals and its reputation for discreet mediation. Bangladesh's foreign ministry welcomed the development, noting that improved India-Pakistan relations would benefit broader regional stability and connectivity initiatives.

Hardliners in both countries have already begun pushing back against the agreement. Nationalist political parties in India have called the concessions on trade a capitulation, while militant groups in Pakistan have threatened to disrupt the process. Security analysts warned that the arrangement remained fragile and vulnerable to a single destabilizing incident.

title: Federal Reserve Holds Rates Steady Amid Global Uncertainty
summary: Fed Chair signals patience as inflation remains above target while labor market shows early signs of softening across key sectors.
content:
The Federal Reserve held its benchmark interest rate unchanged at a target range of 4.25 to 4.50 percent on Wednesday, as policymakers signaled they needed more evidence of sustained disinflation before committing to the rate cuts that financial markets have been anticipating since late last year.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the two-day Federal Open Market Committee meeting, acknowledged that inflation had continued to moderate but said the pace of decline remained insufficient to justify easing monetary conditions. He pointed in particular to stubbornly elevated services inflation, which has proven more resistant to higher rates than the goods sector.

Financial markets responded with modest declines in equity indices and a slight strengthening of the dollar, as traders adjusted expectations for the first rate cut further into the year. Futures pricing now implies a roughly 40 percent probability of a cut at the June meeting, down from over 60 percent a week ago.

The decision comes against a backdrop of growing uncertainty about the global economic outlook, with slowing growth in China, persistent geopolitical disruptions to energy and commodity markets, and ongoing fiscal pressures in several major European economies all weighing on the Fed's assessment.

For Bangladesh and other emerging economies, the Fed's hold is a double-edged development. While higher US rates support the dollar and complicate debt servicing for countries with dollar-denominated obligations, a premature Fed pivot that reignited US inflation could ultimately prove more damaging to global financial stability.

title: EU Parliament Approves Landmark AI Liability Directive
summary: The sweeping regulation makes it easier for citizens to seek compensation when harmed by AI-powered systems, setting a global benchmark for accountability.
content:
The European Parliament voted by a substantial majority on Tuesday to approve the AI Liability Directive, a landmark piece of legislation that will make it significantly easier for European citizens and businesses to seek compensation when they suffer harm caused by artificial intelligence systems.

The directive, which passed 418 votes to 209, introduces a presumption of causality in AI-related damage claims — meaning that plaintiffs no longer need to prove precisely how an AI system caused their harm, only that it was involved and that the developer or deployer failed to meet the applicable standard of care.

Technology industry groups warned that the rules, which will apply to all AI systems operating in EU markets regardless of where they were developed, would impose significant compliance costs and create legal uncertainty that could slow innovation. The directive goes substantially further than similar legislation being developed in the United States and United Kingdom.

Consumer rights organizations, however, hailed the vote as a historic step toward meaningful accountability in an era of increasingly autonomous systems. The directive covers a broad range of AI applications, from recruitment algorithms and credit scoring tools to medical diagnostic systems and autonomous vehicles.

The legislation now moves to the Council of the European Union for final approval before entering into force. Member states will have two years to transpose the directive into national law. Bangladesh's ICT Ministry has already indicated it is monitoring EU AI legislation closely as a reference point for developing its own regulatory framework.
